title: CENTRALLY SPONSORED EDUCATIONAL SCHEMES .
question_text:
(a) the salient features of the centrally sponsored schemes of Joint GOI-UN Programme for Primary Education; Sarvasiksha Abhiyan; National Programme for Women`s Education; Literacy Campaign; Operation Restoration support to NGOs in the field of Adult Education (earlier Shramic Vidyapeeth); Continuing Education for New Literates; Culture Exchange Programme;

(b) what are the criteria for allocating funds under each scheme to States and the corresponding Central share ; whether these funds are given to States as 100 percent grant or the usual 70/30 (loan /grant) formula is followed for Plan assistance; and

(c) the details of proposals received, funds released and utilization for last three years, State-wise?
answer_text:
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

( PROF. RITA VERMA)

(a) The salient features of various centrally sponsored schemes referred to in the Question are as follows:-

1. Janshala (Joint GOI-UN Programme) : It is a community-based primary education programme with the objective of making primary education more accessible and effective, especially for girls and children of deprived communities, marginalised groups, SC/ST/minorities, working children and children with special needs. The programme is a collaborative effort of the Government of India and five UN agencies viz. UNDP, UNICEF, UNESCO, ILO and UNFPA to provide programme support to the ongoing efforts towards achieving Universalisation of Elementary Education. The programme presently covers 139 blocks and 10 cities in 31 districts spread over 9 States i.e. Andhra Pradesh, Chattisgarh, Jharkhand, Karnataka, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Orissa, Rajasthan and Uttar Pradesh. The UN agencies have committed a contribution of US $ 20 million as a grant for this programme.

2. Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA) : It is a newly launched scheme with a holistic and integrated approach to achieve the objective of Universalisation of Elementary Education. The programme aims to provide useful and quality elementary education of 8 years to all children in the 6-14 age group by 2010. The programme will cover all the districts in the country. The expenditure on the programme will be shared between Centre & States in the ratio 85:15 in the 9th Plan, 75:25 in the 10th Plan and 50:50 in the 11th Plan and in the subsequent Plan periods.

3. National Programme for Women’s Education : At present no such scheme is under operation.

4. Literacy Campaigns and Operation Restoration : The Total Literacy Campaign (TLC) is the principal strategy of the National Literacy Mission for eradication of illiteracy. Besides imparting functional literacy, it also include interventions for increasing enrolment and retention of children in schools, immunisation, propagation of small family norms, promotion of maternity and child care, women`s equality and empowerment, peace and communal harmony etc. On conclusion of TLC, Post Literacy Programme (PLP) is taken up for consolidation of literacy skills and integration of skill development programmes. Out of 598 districts in the country, 574 districts are covered under Adult Education programmes. The funding pattern is in the ratio of 2:1 and 4:1 between Centre and State Governments for normal and tribal districts respectively.


5. Continuing Education : The scheme aims at providing further learning opportunities to neo-Literates by setting up Continuing Education Centres, to serve a population of 2000- 2500 by providing facilities of library, reading room, learning centre etc. The Central Government provides 100% funding for the first three years which is to be shared between the Central and State Governments equally during the next two years. The scheme has been sanctioned to 112 districts in 18 States and Union Territories. Recurring assistance is provided to the Nodal and Continuing Education Centres @ Rs. 45,000/- and 25,000/- per year respectively. A non-recurring assistance is also provided on the same scale once in five years.
6. Jan Shikshan Sansthan (formerly known as Shramik Vidyapeeth): The scheme aims to provide polyvalent or multi-faceted adult education programme by improving the vocational skills and quality of life of its beneficiaries, which are mostly neo-literates, semi- literates and non-literates. It concentrates on the socio- economically backward and educationally dis-advantaged groups of urban/rural population, such as neo-literates, semi-literates, SCs, STs, women and girls, slum dwellers, migrant workers, etc.

7 Cultural Exchange Programme: Ministry of Culture is the nodal agency for formulation of Cultural Exchange Programmes and monitoring their implementation. Cultural Exchange Programmes incorporate specific provisions for exchanges such as visits of professors, experts, scholars, artists, writers, performing troupes, sports teams, exhibitions, youth and children`s groups, radio and TV programmes, award of scholarships, training in specialised fields, institutional cooperation and participation in international fairs, exhibitions, festivals etc. They also include items for establishment of institutional links, especially between universities and other institutions of higher education and research. No State-wise releases have been made by this Department under these programmes.

(b) & (c): The entire Central share for the above schemes is released as grant. Funds are released to the concerned States/State Implementation Societies as per the funding pattern of various schemes, progress of expenditure, availability of unspent balances etc. In the case of Janshala (Joint GOI-UN Programme), funds were released by the concerned UN agencies directly to the project States upto March, 2001 and therefore figures of expenditure are reported. In the case of Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA), since the programme was launched during the last financial year only, figures of utilisation of funds are yet to become available. In the case of schemes for Adult Education, unutilised funds during the year are allowed to be carried over to the next financial year. The information relating to funds sanctioned/expenditure on the above lines is given in the Annex. title: SCARCITY OF CHILDREN S LITERATURE .
question_text:
(a) whether it is a fact that Children remain deprived of the Child literature due to its scarcity and they remain ignorant of the culture and traditions of the country;

(b) if so, the reasons therefor; and

(c) whether Government have taken any steps to remove the shortcoming? )
answer_text:
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(PROF. RITA VERMA)

(a)(b)&(c): The Government has been taking a number of steps to boost, support and disseminate Children’s literature in the country through bodies like National Book Trust, India (NBT), Sahitya Akademi, Publications Division, etc. A number of public and private publishers are also making valuable contribution to this cause.
NBT, a premier organization set up by the Government, is operating children specific schemes like Nehru Bal Pustakalaya (NBP) and National Centre for Children’s Literature (NCCL). The NBP series of publications of NBT aims at creating a treasure house of enjoyable and informative literature, which children can read at their own initiative. It promotes national integration by providing common reading material on a wide range of subjects in their mother tongue to children all over India. The NCCL promotes publication of children’s literature. To facilitate awareness about children’s literature, NCCL provides information to guardians, teachers, educationists and planners. In the reference library of the Centre, there are about ten thousand books in 14 Indian languages and 20 foreign languages. NCCL has started ‘Readers Club’ movement to make reading interesting and to make books available for all especially children. The Centre has established about 21,000 Readers’ Clubs in all the States of the country so far. Children are major beneficiaries of these Clubs.
District Primary Education Programme (DPEP) supports school libraries. Mobile book van tours have been conducted in West Bengal, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh in collaboration with NBT. NBT on its own also operates Mobile Exhibition-cum-Sales Vans to make books available to different areas in the country in the States of Delhi, Orissa, Gujarat, Kerala, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Assam.

title: DIGITAL LIBRARY FOR RESEARCH SCHOLARS .
question_text:
(a) when, in what manner, and wherefrom the research scholars students can take advantage of conventional knowledge and digital library;

(b) whether the libraries of all the universities of the country have been linked with this facility;

(c) if so, the details thereof; and

(d) if not, the reasons therefor?
answer_text:
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(PROF. RITA VERMA)

(a) to (d): A statement is laid on the Table of the House.

STATEMENT REFERRED TO IN REPLY TO PARTS (a) TO (d) OF THE RAJYA SABHA STARRED QUESTION NO.644 TO BE ANSWERED ON 10.5.2002 REGARDING DIGITAL LIBRARY FOR RESEARCH SCHOLARS AKSED BY SHRIMATI KUM KUM RAI, MP


(a) to (d): Almost all universities are having large libraries to cater to the requirements of research scholars students. There is a growing realization that conventional knowledge can be made available to all students and researchers by networking the libraries using the information and communication technology. At the national level two Library networks, viz. Information & Library Network (INFLIBNET) and Developing Library Network (DELNET) are emerging,
According to the information furnished by the University Grants Commission (UGC), under the INFLIBNET programme the Commission is funding eligible universities for modernizing libraries. The programme entails creation of electronic database of catalogues of library books/journals/Ph.D./M.Phil. thesis giving titles and authors’ details. This database can be electronically accessed for reference work. UGC proposes to create a “Digital Repository of Research & Teaching material”, termed as a ‘digital library’, to provide access to electronic research journals as a central service. The UGC is also in the process of setting up a UGC Net to connect more than 200 universities and their libraries on education and research network (ERNET).

title: SCHEME TO PROMOTE VOLUNTARY ACTION FOR PERSONS WITH DISABILITIES .
question_text:
(a) the details of NGO`s working to promote voluntary action for persons with disabilities in the country, particularly in Maharashtra;

title: UNIVERSALISATION OF PRIMARY EDUCATION IN M.P. AND CHHATTISGARH .
question_text:
(a) the year-wise amount received during the last three years from Central Government and foreign countries for universalisation of primary education in Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh;

(b) what were the targets fixed therefor;

(c) the details of the heads in which the said amount has been spent; and

(d) the details of the targets achieve through this scheme?
answer_text:
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(PROF. RITA VERMA)

(a) to (d): Presently, the Centrally Sponsored Schemes of the District Primary Education Programme (DPEP), Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan

(SSA), Restructuring & Reorganisation of Teacher Education and Janshala Programme are being implemented in Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh for universalisation of primary education. The schemes of Operation Blackboard and Non-formal Education

(subsequently revised as Education Guarantee Scheme and Alternative & Innovative Education), which were also being implemented in these States, have since been subsumed under SSA from 10th Plan. DPEP in the above states is being assisted by the European Commission and the World Bank and the Janshala Programme is being implemented in collaboration with the five UN agencies namely United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), United Nations Children`s Fund (UNICEF), United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO), International Labour Organisation (ILO) and United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). The position of funds received by Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh during the last three years, i.e. 2000- 2001 to 2002-2003 (upto September, 2002) under the above schemes is given in Annex.I.
The major programme components under which the funds have been utilised and the achievements against project targets in respect of DPEP and Janshala are given in Annex-II. Funds under Operation Blackboard and Non-formal Education Schemes were being provided against the specific proposals received from the States and not against any pre-planned project targets. The major programme components and physical achievements in respect of these schemes are given in Annex.III. Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA) has been launched recently and is at initial stage of implementation.

title: PER CENT LITERACY .
question_text:
(a) the present status of the percentage of literate persons in the country; and

(b) details of steps Government have taken or propose to take for increasing the literate person upto 100 per cent in the future?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

( SHRI ASHOK PRADHAN )

(a) According to Census 2001 the literacy rate in the country is 65.38 per cent.

(b) The 86th Constitutional Amendment Act 2002 provides for free and compulsory education as a fundamental right for all children in the 6-14 age group. The Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan

(SSA) has been launched as a National Programme to implement the fundamental right to free and compulsory education. The main goals of the programme are: · All children in school, Education Guarantee Centre, Alternate School, ‘Back-to-School’ camp by 2003. · All children complete five years of primary schooling by 2007. · All children complete eight years of elementary schooling by 2010. · Focus on elementary education of satisfactory quality with emphasis on education for life. · Bridge all gender and social category gaps at primary stage by 2007 and at elementary education level by 2010. · Universal retention by 2010. The National Literacy Mission seeks to impart functional literacy to non-literates in 15-35 years age-group. The following steps are being taken in this regard :-
i) All uncovered districts would be covered under literacy programme.
ii) Taking up of composite total literacy and post literacy programme.
iii) Special programmes are being taken up to address female illiteracy in low female literacy areas, in addition to the ongoing programmes to promote literacy.
iv) Enlarging the scope of Continuing Education to include skill development programme, individual interest programme, rural libraries and removal of residual illiteracy.
v) State Resource Centres would be strengthened to provide academic and technical resource support to the literacy programmes.
vi) The activities of Jan Shikshan Sansthans would be extended for creation of self-employment opportunities through vocational training programme.

title: DEVLOPMENT OF ARTS AND CULTURE .
question_text:
(a) the achievements made by Government towards promotion and development of arts and culture;

(b) the achievements made and the activities undertaken by Government through cultural centres both in the country and abroad during each of the last three years;

(c) whether Government have set up Cultural Resources and Training Centres for foreigners; and

(d) if so, the details of the functions and objectives thereof and achievements made by them?
answer_text:
MINISTER FOR INFORMATION & BROADCASTING AND CULTURE

( SHRI S. JAIPAL REDDY )

(a) The Ministry of Culture is implementing various schemes for the development and promotion of arts and culture. The Akademies take up the promotion of art and culture in the area of literature, performing arts and visual arts. Institutions for Buddhist Studies under the Ministry as well as specialized Libraries like Rampur Raza Library and Khuda Baksh Oriental Public Library preserve this knowledge and literature. The seven Zonal Cultural Centres work for the promotion of primarily diverse folk culture.

(b) Activities and achievements made by the seven Zonal Cultural Centres during the last three years are as under: 

Year	Number of activities undertaken		Achievements made
2002–03			2003-04			2004-05
643			643			500
Through these programs, the zonal cultural centers are achieving its objectives like preservation, innovation, promotion and dissemination of art forms of the respective zones.  
The Schemes like national Cultural Exchange Programme helps linkages among various areas 
through evolution of styles.  It gives an opportunity for the people of one area to see and understand the different art forms of other zones which inter-alia helps in better 
understanding of Indian unity.  The Guru Shisya Parampara assists the preservation of rare 
and dying art forms.   The Zonal Cultural Centres are also actively involved in the 
documentation of various art forms of respective areas.  During the period under review, two programmes were organized abroad by Zonal Cultural Centres. 


(c) No, Sir. 


(d) Does not arise.

title: REDUCTION IN INTAKE IN TECHNICAL INSTITUTES .
question_text:
(a) whether the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) has decided to reduce the sanctioned intake in technical institutes across the country in the month of June 2005;

(b) if so, the details thereof including the number of technical institutes and the sanctioned intake in each institute during the last five years, State-wise and year- wise and as on date; and

(c) the steps taken for the quality control of the technical institutes?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SHRI M.A.A. FATMI)

(a) & (b):The State-wise details of the seats in technical institutes reduced initially and since restored for the year 2005-06 as given by AICTE are as per Annexure-I. The State- wise data pertaining to the number of engineering degree institutes with intake for the years 2000-01, 2001-02,2002- 03, 2003-04 and 2004-05 is given in Annexure-II.

(c) Changes have been incorporated in the existing approval process with a view, inter-alia, to introduce parameters having bearing on quality of technical education. AICTE is focusing on quality improvements in technical education to make engineering graduates globally competitive and acceptable. Some of the main initiatives include Modernization and Removal of Obsolescence, Research Promotion Scheme, National Facilities in Engineering and Technology with Industrial Collaboration, Industry Institute Partnership, Entrepreneurship Development Programme, AICTE Edusat Scheme, Emeritus Fellowship, Visiting Professorship, Staff Development Programme, Quality Improvement Programme and facilities like National Digital Library in Engineering Science & Technology etc.

title: FUNDS FOR ANCIENT AND HISTORICAL BUILDINGS .
question_text:
(a) whether Twelfth Finance Commission have allocated any funds for upkeep of several ancient and historical buildings in the country;

(b) if so, the details thereof;

(c) whether Government of Maharashtra and Punjab have submitted any action plan to Government in this regard; and

(d) if so, the amount Government have sanctioned/released to each State mentioned above so far during 2005-06 for conservation and protected monuments, till date?
answer_text:
MINISTER FOR URBAN DEVELOPMENT AND CULTURE

( SHRI S. JAIPAL REDDY )

(a) & (b) Twelfth Finance Commission has recommended allocation of Rs.625 Crores among various States for heritage conservation i.e. preservation and protection of historical monuments, archaeological sites, public libraries, museums and archives covering the period between 2005-06 and 2009-10.
The Archaeological Survey of India does not get any allocation from the Finance Commission.

(c) No, Sir.

(d) Out of the budget allocation to Archaeological Survey of India (Ministry of Culture) for conservation of ancient monuments in 2005-06, ASI has allocated Rs.757 Lac in the State of Maharashtra ad Rs.111.14 Lac in the State of Punjab for conservation of centrally protected monuments.

title: IMPROVEMENT OF ENGINEERING COLLEGES .
question_text:
(a) whether AICTE has any plans to encourage the ,existing engineering colleges to improve their standards and also enhance their teaching staff,

(b) whether AICTE would assist the engineering colleges, which are located in rural areas of the country, to get better staff and teaching aids;

(c) whether AICTE would function in the same manner like the UGC and help the technical education;

(d) if so, the details of such plans and policies of AICTE; and

(e) the steps being taken at present in this regard and future plans of AICTE?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SHRI M. A. A. FATMI)

(a) to (e): AICTE has been continuously taking up steps towards improvement of standards in the Engineering colleges/institutes. AICTE already has several schemes and programmes under which assistance is made available to, engineering colleges, including those located in rural areas, for improving their standards and quality of teaching staff`. Some of steps are such as Early Faculty Induction Programme, Emeritus Fellowship Scheme, Visiting Professorship Scheme, Career Awards for Young Teachers Scheme, Seminar/Travel Grant for faculty development, Industry Institution Partnership Facilitation Cell, Research Promotion Scheme, Modernisation and Removal of: Obsolescence Scheme, Indian National Digital Library in Engineering, Science and Technology Programme for research and institutional development. However, mandates and budgetary resources of UGC and AICTE are quite distinct.

title: CHEMES OF THE DEPARTMENT OF CULTURE FOR STATES .
question_text:
(a) the names of the schemes of the Department of Culture in force in the States;

(b) the amount received by the State Governments under various schemes;

(c) the essential feature of each of the schemes and conditions attached to financial assistance; and

(d) the evaluation of implementation of each of the schemes, if done?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF TOURISM AND CULTURE

( SMT. AMBIKA SONI )

(a) & (b) : There are no schemes of the Ministry of Culture under which State Governments receive grant-in-aid. The Grant-in-aid Schemes implemented by the Ministry of Culture benefit cultural organizations/Individuals.

(c) The information is given in Annexure.

(d) The Scheme of Scholarship was got evaluated through Edcil. The effectiveness of other schemes are being undertaken on the eve of the formulation of the XI Plan.

ANNEXURE

ANNEXURE REFERRED TO IN REPLY TO PART (C) OF THE RAJYA SABHA UNSTARRED QUESTION NO.2606 FOR 22.8.2006 GRANTS FROM THE MINISTRY

The Ministry of Culture operates a number of Schemes intended to provide monetary assistance to individuals, groups and voluntary organisations engaged in promoting a particular art group and/ or to sustain cultural activities in the country. The Ministry of Culture provides substantial financial assistance to young individuals who wish to pursue the arts as a profession. Scholarships are awarded to young artists, and later followed by Junior and Senior Fellowships. The evaluation of these schemes has shown that many prominent artistes in the field of dance, painting and music today had, by and large been the recipients either of these scholarships or fellowships offered by the Ministry.

The details of the schemes are given below:

SchemeScholarships to young artistes in different cultural fields. ObjectiveTo give financial assistance to young artistes of outstanding promise for advanced training within India in the fields of music, dance, drama, painting, sculpture, etc.

Applicable toIndian nationals in the age group of 18-25 years having adequate general education to pursue the training effectively, with evidence of a desire to make pursuit of these arts into a professional career, as also a degree of proficiency in the chosen fields of activity, and an adequate knowledge in connected art / discipline.

Financial AssistanceRs. 2000 per month / tenable for a period of 2 year only and no extension is permissible.SchemeSenior / Junior fellowships to Outstanding artistes in the field of performing, literary and Plastic arts.ObjectiveFinancial Support to outstanding artistes in the fields of music, dance theatre, visual arts, literature and traditional forms of folk and indigenous art.

Applicable toArtistes in the age group of 41 Years and above are eligible for the Senior Fellowships. Artistes in the age group of 25-40 years are eligible for Junior Fellowships

Financial AssistanceRs. 12000/ - per month (Senior Fellowships) and Rs. 6000/- per month (Junior Fellowships). Tenable for a period of two years only.

SchemeSenior/Junior Fellowships to outstanding artistes in the field of new areas.

ObjectiveFinancial support to outstanding artistes in the field of new areas such as Indology, Musicology, Cultural economics etc.Applicable toArtistes in the age group of 41 Years and above are eligible for the Senior Fellowships. Artistes in the age of 25 to 40 years are eligible for Junior Fellowships.Financial AssistanceRs. 12000/- per month (Senior Fellowships) and Rs. 6000/ - per month (Junior Fellowships). Tenable for a period of two years only.

There is a specific scheme to give assistance to organizations and monasteries engaged in propagating Buddhist and Tibetan Culture. The details of the scheme are given below:

SchemeDevelopment of Buddhist / Tibetan Culture and Art

ObjectivePropagation and Scientific Development of Buddhist / Tibetan Culture and Tradition and Research in related fields.Applicable toVoluntary Buddhist / Tiebtan, Non-Governmental Organisations including monasteries and individuals. Financial AssistanceA maximum of Rs. 5 lakhs per year for single organisation Maintenance / research Project Rs.1 lakh p.a.For repair, restoration and renovation of ancient monasteries, etc, of historical and cultural importance Rs.1.5 1akh.

Award of fellowship / scholarship by organisations for holding of special courses upto Rs. 5 lakh per annum. Purchase of books under the scheme, documentations, cataloguing upto Rs. 1.25 lakh per annum.Financial assistance given for the construction of hostel building-upto Rs.51 lakh.Salary of teachers where organisation is running a school imparting monastic education-Rs. 7 1aks per annum.

The Ministry of Culture has formulated a scheme for providing grant in aid to voluntary organisations /societies for the development and maintenance of National Memorials. Details of the scheme are given below.

Scheme Grant-in-aid to voluntary organisations / societies for development and maintenance of national memorials.

Objective To commemorate the role of eminent national personalities who have contributed to and played a historic role in the history of our country, to highlight their lives and activities as well as the relevance of their ideas to the Indian heritage.

To interpret and convey the cultural and spiritual values they stood for. To create an awareness of the contributions of such personalities to the nation, and particularly to keep the younger generation informed. Applicable toMemorials set up on the initiative of the Central Government / State Government / Civic Bodies / Voluntary Organisations.Financial Assistance Voluntary Organisations registered under the Societies Registration Act 1860 are eligible for Financial Assistance up to a maximum of Rs. 5 1akhs.

To commemorate the role of eminent national personalities and highlight the lives of those who have contributed and played a historic role in the history of India, and to preserve their memory and propagate their message to the younger generation, special one-time grants are offered to registered voluntary organisations, and state and national bodies. Details of the Scheme are as follows:

SchemeCelebration of Centenaries/Anniversaries of Important Personalities.

ObjectiveTo highlight salient aspects of the life and times of important personalities in order to infuse in the public, particularly the young, the spirit of these great leaders. Applicable toRegistered Voluntary Organisations and State and National bodies for achieving the objectives of the Scheme.Financial Assistance Grants not exceeding Rupees 1 Iakh to Voluntary Organisations for appropriate programmes.

Not exceeding Rs. 40,000 in case of anniversary celebrations of jubilee years i.e.125th, 150th and so on.Grants will not exceed 75% of total expenditure. The remaining 25% of the expenditure is to be borne by the organisation.

The scheme for the preservation and development of cultural heritage of the Himalayas focuses on the study, research, and documentation of the cultural heritage of this region. Details of the scheme are given below.

SchemeFinancial Assistance for Preservation and development of the cultural heritage of the Himalayas ObjectiveTo promote, protect and preserve the cultural heritage of the Himalayas Applicable toInstitutions, individuals and voluntary organisations for their short-duration projects including:

(a) Study and research of all aspects of cultural heritage of Himalayas.

(b) Collection of objects of art and crafts of the Himalayas.

(c) Dissemination of the Himalayan culture through audiovisual programmes.

(d) Assisting and setting up of museums and libraries of Himalayan culture Financial Assistance Grant up to Rs. 5 lakh. The grant is given on the recommendation of an expert committee constituted of for this purpose.

The Scheme below is intended to give a thrust to the promotion and dissemination of folk and tribal art in culture.

Scheme Financial Assistance for Promotion and Dissemination of Tribal / Folk Art and Culture.

Objective The Scheme will address itself mainly to the well-identified and urgent need for the preservation, promotion and dissemination of Tribal and Rural Art and Culture. Its objective are-

(a) To provide the tribal with opportunities to conduct their cultural activities in their environment and to collect and preserve their arts and crafts in their milieu so as to ensure the continuity to the Tribal Arts and Crafts.

(b)To promote and support documentation, research and survey.

(c)To assist the educational authorities of State Government concerned in identifying project, which will help to integrate the educational system in Tribal areas with the cultural tradition of the Tribal and rural communities.

(d)Disseminate the awareness of the riches of Tribal/Rural culture, particularly amongst urban educated people.

(e)To promote the preservation and the development of tribal arts and crafts and other facets of tribal culture by all other means. Applicable to Voluntary Organisation / Individuals engaged in preservation and the promotion of tribal / folk art culture. Financial Assistance The maximum grant envisaged under the scheme is Rs. 2 1akh for a project.

A scheme to give financial assistance to voluntary organisations engaged in cultural activities. i.e. literary, visual and performing arts is given below:

Scheme Financial Assistance for Research support to Voluntary Organisations engaged in cultural activities.

Objective To meet expenditure on activities of development nature and for holding of conference, seminars and symposia on important cultural matters. Applicable to Voluntary Organisations of an all- India character and of national fame registered under Societies Registration Act (XI of 1860) and engaged in cultural activities for at least 3 years are eligible for the grant; such organisations or institutions as are functioning as religious institution, public libraries, universities or school are not eligible for grant under the scheme. Financial Assistance The amount of financial assistance shall be restricted to 75% of the expenditure subject to maximum Rs.1lakh. The applications recommended by the State Government / State Akademis are considered by the Expert Committee.

A special scheme has been formulated by the department to give building grants to private non-governmental organisations engaged in the service of cultural dissemination with a view to creating permanent centres of cultural interactions and learning. The details are given below-

Scheme Financial Assistance for Building and equipment grants to voluntary cultural organisations.

Objective To give grants to voluntary cultural organisations primarily working in the fields of dance, drama, music, fine arts, Indology and literature for construction of buildings and purchase of equipments. Applicable to

(a) All organisations excluding those run by local bodies, municipalities/corporations. Such organisations must primarily function in cultural fields and must have been functioning for at least five years and registered at least for a period of two years under the Registrations of Societies Act (XXI) of 1860. For similar Acts and the organisations that are recommended by the state governments or Union Territory administrations.

(b) The organisation must be or regional or all-India character.

(c) Its work must be substantially devoted to programmes that deal with preservation, propagation and promotion of Indian culture. Financial Assistance The maximum assistance admissible to an organisation will be 50 per cent of the expenditure subject to maximum of Rs. 15 lakh for the construction of building. (The quantum of assistance does not exceed 50 per cent of the estimated cost of the project). The quantum of assistance for equipments shall not exceed Rs. 2.5 lakh. Applications under the scheme are to be routed through the Departments of Culture of the concerned State Governments / Union Territories and are to be considered by an expert committee.

Aged or incapacitated artists are rendered Financial support up to a maximum of Rs.2000/- per month through a scheme, details of which are given below. Under this scheme there is also a provision to look after the artists’ dependents till a certain age limit and medical aid is provided to them.

Scheme Financial Assistance to Persons distinguished in Letters and Art whose dependants may be in indigent circumstances.

Objective To support and sustain old and poor artists. Applicable to People (aged 58 or above) distinguished in letters and arts who may be in indigent circumstances and whose monthly income does not exceed Rs. 2000/ - per month Financial Assistance Financial Assistance from the Government may be in the form of monthly allowance.Such allowance is given to artists recommended under the Centre-State / UT quota and will be shared by the Centre and State / UT Government concerned in the ration 3: 1. However, the monthly allowance contributed by the Central Government in such cases shall not exceed Rs. 1500/ - per month, and in cases of those under Central quota shall not exceed Rs. 2000/ - per month. The artists are recomm ended for assistance by an expert committee constituted for the purpose.

Financial Assistance is provided to an autonomous body created by State Governments to set up art and cultural complexes. Cultural complexes for Children as are also considered on a case-to-case basis. Details of the scheme are given below :

Scheme Financial Assistance for Setting up of Multi-purpose Cultural Complexes, including those for Children

Objective To promote, disseminate and support excellence in creative arts, literature etc. and to improve the sensibilities of the younger generation by exposing them to the finest forms in different fields of art and culture. Applicable to Autonomous bodies registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 and set up by a State Government. Financial Assistance Assistance will be given for two categories of MPCCs :
i) One project in each State costing upto Rs. 5.00 crores will be considered for assistance upto 50% of the project cost.
ii) In the case of smaller projects assistance upto Rs. 1.00 crore would be admissible. In both cases land will be provided free of cost by the State Government and also all recurring expenditure will be borne by the State Government. In the case of North Eastern States (including Sikkim), Centre’s assistance will be in the ratio of 90 : 10. For all other States, Centre’s assistance will be in the ratio of 50 : 50.

title: FORMULATION OF A NATIONAL POLICY OF PUBLIC LIBRARY AND INFORMATION SERVICES. .
question_text:
(a) whether it is a fact that for formulating a National Policy of Public Library and Information Services, Government have set up a Committee headed by Prof. D. P. Chattopadhyaya;

(b) if so, whether the aforesaid Committee had submitted its Report by 1986;

(c) if so, what effective follow-up measures have been taken by the Government; and

(d) whether Government are contemplating to declare a much-awaited National Policy of Public Library and Information Services?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF TOURISM AND CULTURE

(SMT. AMBIKA SONI)

(a) Yes, Sir. Government had set up a Committee headed by Prof. D. P. Chattopadhyaya on October 7, 1985.

(b) Yes, Sir.

(c & d) An Empowered Committee to formulate an Action Plan on the National Policy on Library and Information System and a Working group on the decisions of the Empowered Committee were set up. Guidelines for providing financial support to individuals, libraries and voluntary organizations in Library sector have been issued. However, a National Policy of Public Library and Information has not been declared.

title: Status of Dr. B.R.  Ambedkar Foundation
question_text:
(a) the status of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar Foundation under the administrative control of her Ministry;

(b) the details of present activities of the foundation;

(c) the present status of National Memorial of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar at New Delhi; and

(d) the reasons due to which things are moving at very slow speed?
answer_text:
ANSWER

MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF SOCIAL JUSTICE AND EMPOWERMENT

(SMT. SUBBULAKSHMI JAGADEESAN)

(a) The Dr. Ambedkar Foundation is a Registered Society under the Societies Registration Act 1860.

(b) The details are given in the Annexure.

(c)&(d) The Museum at 26, Alipur Road, Delhi consists of two galleries. The endeavour of the Foundation is to augment the collections and belongings of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, for which an `Appeal` has also been made through newspapers.

Annexure to part(b) of the Rajya Sabha Unstarred Question No. 2082, for on 14.8.06, by Shri Praveen Rashtrapal, regarding `Status of Dr. Ambedkar Foundation`. 



-	Maintenance of Dr. Ambedkar National Memoriat at 26, Alipur Road, New 
       Delhi
-	Setting up of Dr. Ambedkar National Public Library at Janpath, New Delhi.
-	Dr. Ambedkar National Award for Social Undertstanding and Upliftment of 
	Weaker Sections.
-	Dr. Ambedkar International Award for Social Change.
-	Dr. Ambedkar Chairs in Universities/Institutions for carrying out     
       research on 
	the thoughts and ideology of Babasahe Dr. B.R.Ambedkar.
-	Publication of the Collected Works of Babasaheb into Hindi and other 
	regional languages.
-	Dr. Ambedkar National Merit Scholarship Scheme to Meritorious Scheduled 
	Castes and Scheduled Tribe students.
-	Organisation of seminars, symposia, conferences, workshops, lectures, 
	exhibitions and fairs on the life and mission of Dr. Ambedkar.
-	Celebration of  Birth Anniversary and Mahaparinirwan Diwas of Dr. 
	Ambedkar on 14th  April and 6th December respectively  every year.
-	Dr. Ambedkar Medical Aid Scheme.
-	Dr. Ambedkar Samajik Samta Kendra Yojna.
-	Celebrations of Birth Anniversaries of Great Saints.
-	Publication of a monthly magazine `Samajik Nyaya Sandesh`.

title: SCHEME FOR SOCIO ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT AND EDUCATIONAL ADVANCEMENT OF TRIBALS .
question_text:
(a) the schools/schemes being run by NGOs is the scheduled areas of Andhra Pradesh, district-wise and NGO-wise for socio-economic development and educational advancement of tribal people;

(b) the details of grant-in-aid given to NGOs of Andhra Pradesh during the last three years;

(c) The details of the cases of misuse of funds, if any, by NGOs under any Centrally-sponsored tribal development programme indicating the action taken against the erring NGOs; and

(d) the details of the new schemes, if any, for advancement of tribal/hill people of the state ?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF TRIBAL AFFAIRS

(SHRI P.R. KYNDIAH)

(a) & (b) In the Scheduled Areas of Andhra Pradesh, the Ministry of Tribal Affairs has been implementing the following schemes through voluntary organizations for the socio-economic development and educational advancement of Scheduled Tribes:

(1) Grant in aid to Voluntary Organizations working for the Welfare of Scheduled Tribes;

(2) Educational Complexes in low literacy pockets for Scheduled Tribe girls;

(3) Development of Primitive Tribal Groups.
The district-wise details of grant-in-aid released to the NGOs of Andhra Pradesh during the last three years under these schemes are given at Annexure.

(c) No such instance has come to the notice of the Ministry during the last three years.

(d) The existing schemes of the Ministry are meant for all tribal people including those living in hilly areas and there is no proposal for a new scheme.

title: RESTORATION OF CONNEMARA PUBLIC LIBRARY BUILDING IN CHENNAI .
question_text:
(a) whether the ASI has any proposal to restore the world famous Connemara Public Library Building, Chennai and other places of importance in Tamil Nadu; and

(b) if so, the details thereof including the estimated cost of projects and estimated time for completion, project-wise?
answer_text:
MINISTER FOR TOURISM AND CULTURE

(SMT. AMBIKA SONI)

(a) &(b) The Statement is laid on the table of the House.

STATEMENT REFERRED TO IN REPLY TO PART (a) TO (b) OF THE RAJYA SABHA STARRED QUESTION NO. 390 FOR 19.12.2006


(a) &(b) Archaeological Survey of India has restored the Connemara Public Library Building at Chennai as a Deposit Work. The major items of restoration carried out included;Ø relaying of damaged tiled roof in central portion and Madras terrace roof on the aisle portion,Ø mending and replacement of missing pieces of stained glasses with floral designs on vaulted roof and wall panels,Ø restoration of stucco work and architectural features inside the building, and Ø restoration of damaged art work on the ceiling.The total cost of restoration is Rs.1,02,21,079/-.The details of the conservation / restoration works undertaken by the Archaeological Survey of India in Tamilnadu during 2006-07 is at annexure.

title: CONFERENCE OF EDUCATION MINISTERS OF STATES .
question_text:
(a) the decision taken in the conference of Ministers of States concluded recently;

(b) whether his Ministry have decided to open a Central university in all the States of the country;

(c) the names of the States which don`t have any Central university, so far;

(d) whether this`, decision has also been taken that Government would provide a Central university level financial assistance to at least one State-level university through U.G.C. in States which don`t have any Central university; and

(e) whether the conference also took into consideration the proposal of extending Mid-Day Meal Scheme up to eighth standard?
answer_text:
MN1STER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SMT. D. PURANDESWARI)

a) A Conference all State Education Ministers was held on 10th and 11th April 2007 at New Delhi. Record of the Consensus Points reached in the Conference is annexed.

(b to d) A decision was taken that one State University in each State which does not have any Central University al: present may be funded to the level of a Central University, through the UGC, subject to availability of resources. However, the Planning Commission is yet to finalise the Eleventh Five Year Plan for the Ministry. At present Bihar, Chhatisgarh, Goa, Gujarat, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir, Jharkhand, Karnataka~ Kerala, Madhya Pradesh, Orissa, Punjab, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu and Utthrakhand do not have any Central University.
e) Yes, Sir. The Government of India will re-.formulate the parameters of the Mid-Day Meal Scheme, such ~that the entire upper primary stage is covered by the end of the Eleventh Five Year Plan.

ANNEXURE

CONFERENCE OF STATE E:DUCATION MINISTERS NEW DELHI:APRIL 10-11, 2007
Record of the Consensus Points 

 

1.	General


	The HRD Ministry and State Education Departments will work intensively 
with other concerned authorities and Departments at the Central and State levels 
so as to increase public expenditure on Education to the level of 6% of GDP, by 
the end of the Xl Plan period. Also, the National Development Council is urged 
to evolve a oonsensus in this regard to the manner of achieving this goal at the 
earliest.



	The implementation of the Central Educational institutions (Reservation 
in Admission) Act, which was enacted and notified in January, 2007 has, by an 
interim order of the Hon`ble Supreme Court, been kept in abeyance. The 
Conference noted that the Central Government is examining all legal options to 
resolve this issue at the earliest.



	States agreed to speed up enactment of State-specific legislations to 
give effect to the 93rd Constitutional Amendment which enables reservations for 
SCs, STs and Socially and Educationally Backward Classes in educational 
institutions. State Governments urged the Central Government to ensure that the 
reservations for the OBCs in the Central Educational Institutions was 
implemented from the forthcoming academic session, this year itself.



	Education Ministers of the State Governments were of the opinion that the 
recommendations of the National Knowledge Commission (NKC) were unrealistic ,or 
undesirable from the point of view of enhancing access and equity in Higher and 
Technical Education, It was resolved that the recommendations of the NKC may be 
considered by the States only after a view in regard to these 
	recommendations have been taken by the Central Government.



	Central Regulatory bodies like UGC, AICTE and NCTE should give due 
weightage to the views and concerns of State Governments. Policies and statutory 
provisions relating to these bodies should also be reviewed for this purpose, 
where necessary.



	Education Ministers of States where there is a ban on recruitment of 
teachers / regular teachers, agreed to make assiduous efforts to get this ban 
lifted at the earliest 1:o enable filling up of vacant posts on a regular basis.
Every effort will be made to make buildings of all existing educational 
institutions - schools, colleges and universities - barrier-free, so as to 
provide full access to disabled students, latest by the end of the XI Plan. 
Also, all new buildings will be constructed so as to be barrier-free, 
differentially-abled friendly, eco-friendly and energy-efficient.



	States noted that work of authentication of educational certificates 
would cease to be done at the level of HRD Ministry with effect from 30.6.07, 
States which have not yet established necessary mechanisms for undertaking this 
work, agreed to do so latest by that date.



2.	Elementary Education


The conference endorsed the view that the issue of the Centre-Statefunding 
pattern in the SSA to be continued on a 90:10 ratio for theNE States and on a 
75:2:5 ratio for other States be examined appropriately by the Government of 
India.



	A consensus emerged in the meeting to continue the implementation of the 
SSA programme with vigour & commitment, and with the fullest attention to equity 
issues; improvement of learning outcomes of students; implementing focused 
programmes for improving language and mathematics basic levels in early primary 
grades and tracking student as welt as teacher attendance rates at elementary 
levels.



	 State Ministers agreed on the need to consolidate the initiatives taken 
over the past 2-1/2 years on the Mid Day Meal programme and t.o streamline 
delivery systems to ensure better management and distribution of food-grains and 
other commodities required for serving a hot cooked meal~ States are required to 
put in place mechanisms that would ensure that schools are not deprived of 
timely financial assistance to meet the daily requirements of running the 
programme. State Ministers expressed apprehension that, in the expansion of the 
Mid Day Meal Programme to the upper primary stage in educationally backward 
blocks, the current parameters [or identifying such blocks could exclude some of 
the poorer and backward blocks in the country. The Government of India will re-
formulate the parameters such that the entire upper primary stage is covered by 
the end of the XI Plan.




3,    Adult Education


	It was agreed to work towards the target of achieving 85% literacy by the 
end of the 1lth Plan, and to restore the Importance which the National Literacy 
Mission had enjoyed in the 1990s. State Governments were urged to complete their 
literacy programmes in a time bound manner so that the districts can move into 
the continuing education phase as soon as possible. They were also requested to 
bring about convergence between Continuing Education and other major programmes 
like National Rural Health Mission and National Rural Employment Guarantee 
Programme.



	Some States raised the issue of suitable remuneration for volunteers. The 
Central Government is examining this issue.


4.	Secondary Education


	With the unversalisation of elementary education nearing completion, the 
imperative of a Scheme for Universalisation of, Secondary Education to be 
implemented in a mission mode was appreciated by all. It was, however, 
recognized that the contours ofthe Scheme would only be finalized once the 11th 
Plan exercise was completed. 
Pending this, 1311 State Governments and Union Territories were requested to set 
up a Task Force so as to identify gaps in the secondary education sector, 
particularly in terms of access and infrastructure.


  

5.	Higher Education



	The Conference endorsed the target of increasing Gross Enrolment Ratio 
(GER) for Higher Education from the present about 10% to at least 15%, by the 
end of the XI Plan, with minimization of gender and social category gaps. States 
which are significantly below the national average at present in regard to GER, 
agreed to make special efforts to come upto the national average, with suitable 
assistance from the Central Government to correct regional imbalances in 
tertiary education.



	State which does not at present At least one State University in each 
have any Central University may be funded to the level of a Central University, 
through the UGC.



	State Governments will take urgent steps to avail of various schemes of 
UGC assistance, incl. for women`s hostels, so as to take maximum possible 
advantage of the enhanced allocation available for State Universities with the 
UGC. These steps would include, inter alia, the following:



		Pro-actively encouraging State Universities, which are yet to 
apply for recognition under section 12 B of UGC Act, 1956, to do so now so as to 
make them eligible for funding by the UGC. UGC would prepare a fast-track 
mechanism to ensure that all applications from such universities are processed 
on priority.



		As temporary affiliation by universities renders colleges 
ineligible for UGC	 assistance all State Governments will make efforts to 
facilitate early grant of permanent affiliation to maximum possible number of 
colleges  especially those located in rural, scheduled, and minority 
concentration areas - so that they can avail of development assistance from UGC°



	In the XI plan UGC should revamp its scheme to promote job
	oriented courses, so as; to widen its scope and coverage, and to
	make it more effective.


	State Governments will also encourage all their universities and

colleges to go in for accreditation by NAAC, and in regard to technical 
education courses by NBA.



	It was agreed that technical education institutions applying for 
accreditation to the National Board of Accreditation (NBA) should not be 
expected to bear any costs in this regard, except for the prescribed 
accreditation fees, and that all expenses connected with the inspection visit of 
the NBA team should be borne by the NBA t AICTE.



	Efforts will be, made to network all institutions of higher and technical 
education so that collaborative research and shared experiences strengthen our 
tertiary education.



	State Governments wilt also advise their universities to adopt / adapt 
LIGC-approved curricula and to switch over to semester and credit systems, 
within two years.



	States agreed to encourage their universities / colleges and technical 
education institutions to become members of INFLIBNET (Information for Library 
Network) and INDEST (Indian National Digital Library for Engg. Sciences and 
Technology) consortia, respectively, so that they gain access to e.-journals and 
other online academic resources through the centralized subscription process of 
these consortia.


	It was agreed that UGC should give development aid on preferential basis 
to institutions which are located in backward rural areas or cater to 
minorities, women or other disadvantaged sections. It was also agreed that UGC 
should work out suitable parameters for calculating the Diversity Index of the 
various institutions and provide rewards by way of enhanced developmental and 
other grants to institutions which score high on the Diversity Index, taking 
into account both recruitment and enrolment.




6.	Technical Education


	States whose intake capacity in technical education courses per lakh 
population is significantly less than the national average, agreed to initiate 
necessary/ policies and programmes in the XI Plan so as to come up to the level 
of the national average.

	States agreed to make efforts to set up, by the end of the XI Plan, a 
polytechnic in each of those Districts which do not have a polytechnic at 
present, but are large enough to sustain one. They also agreed to consider 
setting up Women`s Polytechnics in such specially identified districts as have 
no separate women`s polytechnic at present. The Ministry will work towards 
providing financial assistance of nonrecurring nature for this purpose.



	State Governments will advise their Universities to update / revise 
`their curricula in technical education in consultation with AICTE, and also to 
switch over to semester and credit-based grading system.




	AICTE should be asked to review the norms fixed for polytechnics as also 
for introduction of new subjects so as to encourage private public 
participation.

AICTE should also be asked to develop suitable mechanisms for meaningful and 
effective consultations with the State Governments.




	The issue of having a minimum percentage of marks in the qualifying 
examination for admissions in the technical institutions was raised by the State 
Governments. It was noted that the AICTE Regulations in this regard do not 
specify such a percentage arid all those who pass the qualifying examination are 
eligible to appear in the entrance examinations conducted for admissions to 
various technical institutions. It was agreed that the AICTE would examine the 
feasibility of imposing a condition of obtaining a minimum percentage of marks 
in the qualifying examinations for entry in the technical institutions.



7    Vocational Education




	Creation /up gradation of facilities which haw~ a bearing on 
skills/training

	across all sectors of education need to be vigorously pursued. This would

	include, inter-alia, participation of trade/ industry/employer 
organizations in

	curriculum design and certification. A national framework of 
qualification for

	skills training would be devised.



8.	Distance Education




	States agreed to liberally make available premises and other 
infrastructure of their institutions to serve as study-centres/regional centres 
of IGNOU, and to permit teachers in their institutions of higher learning to 
take up the assigned work in IGNOU Study Centres subject to the condition that 
their no(real work should not be affected and IGNOU compensates the institutions 
at least to the extent of variable costs, besides giving some remuneration for 
the extra work done by the teachers.




	States noted the need to quickly enter into tripartite, MoU with ISRO and 
MHRD for` proper utilization of EDUSAF~ All States which are yet to execute 
these MoUs, agreed to do so in the next two months. States which have already 
executed the MoUs agreed to intensify efforts for providing infrastructural and 
administrative facilities for installation and maintenance of Satellite 
Interactive Terminals (SITs) and Receive Only Terminals (ROTs).




	States welcomed the establishment of the `SAKSHAT` Education, Portal by 
the Ministry, and agreed to host it on State-level servers as well, and also to 
develop and post on this portal, learning modules in their respective languages, 
and suited to the State-specific context.



9. Minority Education




	The Scheme of introduction of modern subjects in Madrassa institutions, 
being a demand-driven scheme, needs to be widely publicized with the active 
involvement of the State Governments. Special campaign would be launched by the 
MHRD, in association with State; Governments, for willing Madrassa institutions 
to volunteer for assistance under the Scheme. The National Institute of Open 
Schooling (NIOS) would launch a programme of linking with willing Madrassa 
Institutions for imparting education in modern subjects and vocational education 
through open and distance learning mode, leading to appropriate certification of 
eligible and successful Madrassa students by the NIOS.

title: LITRACY UNDER ADULT EDUCATION .
question_text:
(a) the number of people: who have turned literates so far under adult education;

(b) the criterion of imparting adult education and the details of levels at which it is being run;

(c) the amount allocated under adult education during the laser five years;

(d) whether the amount allocated is being properly utilized;

(e) whether there is any monitoring committee for the same, State.-wise; and

(f) the details thereof?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MIN][STRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SHRI M.A.A. FATMI)

(a): According to the information received from the State Governments/Union Territory Administrations, 124.27 million persons are reported to have been made literate since the launching of the National Literacy Mission (NLM)in 1988.

(b): The main criterion under NLM for adult education is to impart functional literacy to non-literates in the 15-35 age group. The programmes of adult education include Total Literacy Campaigns, Post Literacy Programme and Continuing Education Programme. Under Total Literacy Campaigns, literacy is imparted through three specially designed primers over a period of 200 hours. In order to declare a person literate, specific competencies have been prescribed for reading, writing and numeracy. A person has to score 50% in each of these competencies and 70% in aggregate to be declared literate.
Post Literacy Programme focuses on reinforcement of literacy skills so that the neo-literate is able to make a transition from guided learning to self-reliant level of learning. Thereafter, the Continuing Education Prograrame provides facilities of learning centres, library, reading room, sports & cultural activities and other individual interest promotion programmes.

(c) to (f): Under the Adult Education Programme, funds are not allocated State-wise but are released to the implementing agencies depending upon the proposals received from them, completion of the earlier programmes sanctioned to them and settlement of accounts. A statement showing State-wise amount of the grants released under various schemes of adult education to the respective implementing agencies during the last five years is enclosed as Annexure.
The proper utilization of the grants released is ensured through the progress reports, field visits and. scrutiny of the audited accounts and utilisation certificates furnished, by the implementing agencies. Further, the Directorates of Adult Education in the State Governments and State Literacy Mission Authorities regularly monitor the programmes. Regional Review meetings are also held from tirne to time to assess the effectiveness of the literacy programmes being; implemented in various parts of the country.

title: REPRESENTATION FROM TEACHERS ASSOCIATION .
question_text:
(a) whether Government have received any representation/memorandum from Delhi University Teachers` Association (DUTA) during the last three years regarding their demands;

(b) whether Government have taken any action on their memorandum; and

(c) if so, the details thereof?.
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

( SMT. D.PURANDESWARI )

(a) to (c): The Government have been receiving representations/memorandum of demands relating to service conditions from various Associations of Teachers in including the All India Federation of University and College Teachers` Organisation, Federation of Central Universities `Teachers` Association and Delhi University Teachers` Association. The Government have already accepted the major demands of these Associations which, inter-alia, include revised Career Advancement Scheme (CAS) for Assistant Librarian/ College Librarian, Assistant Director of Physical Education/College Director of Physical Education, enhancement of age of superannuation from 62 to 65 years for teaching positions in Centrally funded Institutions in Higher and Technical Education and creation of posts of Professors in the UGC recognized Autonomous Colleges. The Government have also initiated action for setting up of the UGC Pay Review Committee following the constitution of the Sixth Central Pay Commission for the Central Government employees.

title: CONFERENCE OF EDUCATION MINISTERS OF STATES .
question_text:
a) whether a conference of the Education Ministers of States was recently held in New Delhi;

(b) if so, the decisions taken therein, particularly those which impressed upon the Central Government to increase their share of assistance for various educational schemes trader way;

(c) the reaction of the Central Government in each case; and d) by when Central Government are likely to take a decision thereon? ANS WER MINISTER OF HUMAN RESOURCE ]DEVELOPMENT (SHRI ARJUN SINGH)

(a) to

(d) : A Statement is laid. on the Table of the House. STATEMENT REFERRED TO IN REPLY TO PART

(a) TO

(d) OF RAJYA SABHA STARRED QUESTION NO.400 ASKED BY SHRI RAJEEV CHANDRASEKHAR REGARDING CONFERENCE OF EDUCATION MINSITERS OF STATES TO BE
answer_text:
ED ON 30.4.2007. 

 

(a): Yes, Sir.	A Conference of the State Education Ministers was held on 10th and
11th April, 2007.


(b): Points on which a consensus was reached in the said Conference are given in the Annexure. The conference inter-alia resolved that the issue of Centre-State funding pattern for Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan to be continued on a 90:10 ratio for the North Eastern States, should on a 75:25 ratio f3r other States, should be examined appropriately by the Government of India.


(c) & (d): Central Government has initiated appropriate action on the various consensus points for which Central Government is responsible.




ANNEXURE REFERRED IN PART (b) OF THE STATEMENT OF THE RAJYA SABHA STARRED QUESTION NO.400 ASKED BY SHRI RAJEEV CHANDRASEKHAR REGARDING CONFERENCE OF` EDUCATION MINSI`IERS OF STATES TO BE ANSWERED ON 30.4.2007.


Record of the Consensus Points


1.	General


(i)	The HRD Ministry and State Education Departments will work intensively with other concerned authorities and Departments at the Central and State levels so as to increase public expenditure on Education to the level of 6% of GDP, by the end of the XI Plan period. Also, the National Development Council is urged to evolve a
consensus in this regard to the manner of achieving this goal at the earliest.


(ii)	The implementation of the Central Educational Institutions (Reservation in
Admission) Act, which was enacted and notified in January, 2007 has, by an interim order of the Hon`ble Supreme Court, been kept in abeyance. The Conference noted that the Central Government: is examining all legal options to resolve this issue at the earliest.


(iii)	States agreed to speed up enactment of State-specific legislations to give effect to
the 93~d Constitutional Amendment which enables reservations for SCs, STs and Socially and Educationally Backward Classes in educational institutions. State Governments urged the Central Government to ensure that the reservations for the OBCs in the Central Educational Institutions was implemented from the forthcoming academic session, this year itself. 

(iv)	Education Ministers of the State Governments were of the opinion that the recommendations of the ]National Knowledge Commission (NKC) were unrealistic or undesirable from the point of view of enhancing access and equity in Higher and Technical Education. It was resolved that the recommendations of the NKC may
be considered by the States only after a view in regard to these recommenda1:ions
have been taken by the Central Government. 

(v)  Central Regulatory bodies like UGC, AICTE and NCTE should give due weight age    
to  the views and concerns of State Governments. Policies and statutory provisions      
relating to these bodies should also be reviewed for this purpose, where necessary. 

(vi)	Education Ministers of States where there is a, ban on recruitment of teachers /
regular teachers, agreed to make assiduous efforts to get this ban lifted at the
earliest to enable filling up of vacant posts on a regular basis.


(vii)	Every effo11: will be made to make buildings of all existing educational institutions
	-schools, colleges ,and universities - barrier-free, so as to provide full access to
	disabled students, latest by the end of the XI Plan. Also, all new buildings will be
	constructed so as to be barrier-free, differentially-abled friendly, eco-friendly and
	energy-efficient. 

(viii)	States noted that work of authentication of educational certificates would cease to
	be done at the level of HRD Ministry with effect from 30.6.07. States which have
	not yet established necessary mechanisms for undertaking this work, agreed to do
	so latest by that date.


2.	Elementary Education


(i)	The conference endorsed the view that the issue of the Centre-State funding
	pattern !in the SSA to be continued on a 90:10 ratio for the NE States and on a
	75:25 ratio for other States be examined appropriately by the Government of
	India. 

(ii)	A consensus emerged in the meeting to continue the implementation of the SSA
	programme with vigour & commitment, and with the fullest attention to equity
	issues; improvement of learning outcomes of students; implementing focused
	programmes for improving language and mathematics basic levels in early primary
	grades and tracking student as well as teacher attendance rates at elementary levels. 

(iii)	State Ministers agreed on the need to consolidate the initiatives taken over the past 2-1/2 years on the Mid Day Meal programme and to streamline delivery systems to ensure better management and distribution of food grains and other commodities required for serving a hot cooked meal. States are required to put in place mechanisms that would ensure that schools are not deprived of timely financial assistance to meet the daily requirements of running the programme. 

(iv)	State Ministers expressed apprehension that, in the expansion of the Mid Day Meal
	Programme to the upper primary stage in educationally backward blocks, the 	current parameters for identifying such blocks could exclude some of the poorer
	and backward blocks in the country. The Government of India will re-formulate
	the parameters such that the entire upper primary stage is covered by the end of the
	XI Plan.


3.	Adult Education


(i)	It was agreed to work towards the target of achieving 85% literacy by the end of
	the 11th Plan, and to restore the importance which the National Literacy Mission
	had enjoyed in the 1990s. State, Governments were urged to complete their
	literacy programmes in a time bound manner so that the ,districts can move into
	the continuing education phase as soon as possible. They were also requested to
	bring about: convergence between Continuing Education and other major
	programmes like National Rural Health Mission and National Rural Employment
	Guarantee Programme.


(ii)	Some States raised the issue of suitable remuneration for volunteers. The Central Government is examining this issue.


4.	Secondary Education


With the universalisation of elementary education nearing completion, the imperative of a Scheme for Universatisation of Secondary Education to be implemented in a mission mode was appreciated by all. It was  however, recognized that the contours of the Scheme would only be finalized once the 1lth Plan exercise was completed. Pending this, all State Governments and Union Territories were requested to set up a Task Force so as to identify gaps in the secondary education sector, particularly in terms of access and infrastructure.

  

5.    Higher Education


(i)	The Conference endorsed the target of increasing Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) for Higher Education from the present about 10% to at least 15%, by the end of the XI Plan, with minimization of gender and social category gaps. States which are significantly below the national average at present in regard to GER, agreed to make special efforts to come upto the national average, with suitable assistance from the Central Government to correct regional1 imbalances in tertiary education. 

(ii)	At least one State University in each State which does not at present have my Central University may be funded to the level of a Central University, through the UGC. 

(iii)	State Governments will take urgent steps to avail of various schemes of UGC assistance, incl. for women`s hostels, so as to take maximum possible advantage of the enhanced allocation available for State Universities with the UGC. These steps would include, inter alia, the following:


	Pro-actively encouraging State Universities, which are yet to apply for recognition under section 12 B of UGC Act, 1956, to do so now so as to make them eligible for funding by the UGC. UGC would prepare a fast-track mechanism to ensure that all applications from such universities are processed on priority.


	As temporary affiliation by universities renders colleges ineligible for UGC 	assistance, all State Governments will make efforts to facilitate early grant 	of permanent affiliation to maximum possible number of colleges - 	especially those located in rural., scheduled, and minority concentration 	areas - so that they can avail of development assistance from UGC.


(iv)	In the XI plan UGC should revamp its scheme to promote job oriented courses, so as to widen its scope and coverage, and to make it more effective. 

(v)	State Governments will also encourage all their universities and colleges to go in for accreditation by NAAC,, and in regard to technical education courses by NBA.


	It was agreed that technical education institutions applying for accreditation to the National Board of Accreditation (NBA) should not be expected to bear, any costs in this regard, except for the prescribed accreditation fees, and that all expenses connected with the inspection visit of the NBA team should be borne by the NBA/ AICTE. 

(vi)	Efforts will be made to network all institutions of higher and technical education so that collaborative research and ,shared experiences strengthen our tertiary education. 

(vii)	State Governments will also advise their universities to adopt / adapt UGC-approved curricula and to switch over to semester and credit systems, within two years. 

(viii)	States agreed to encourage their universities / colleges and technical education institutions to become members of INFLIBNET (Information for Library Network:) and INDEST (Indian National Digital Library for Engg. Sciences and Technology) consortia, respectively, so that the3 gain access to e-journals and other online academic resources through the centralized subscription process of these consortia.




(ix)	It was agreed that UGC should give development aid on preferential basis to
institutions which are located in backward rural areas or cater to minorities, women or other disadvantaged sections. It was also agreed that UGC should work out suitable parameters for calculating the Diversity Index of the various institutions and provide,, rewards by way of enhanced developmental and other grants to institutions which score high on the Diversity Index, taking into account both recruitment and enrolment.


6.	Technical Education


(i)	States whose intake capacity in technical education courses per lakh population is
Significantly less than the national average, agreed to initiate necessary policies and programmes in the XI Plan so as to come up to the level of the national average. 

(ii)	States agreed to make efforts to set up, by the end of the XI Plan, a polytechnic in each of those Districts which do not have a polytechnic at present, but are large enough to sustain one. They also agreed to consider setting up Women`s Polytechnics in such specially identified districts as have no separate women`s polytechnic at present. The Ministry will work towards providing financial assistance of non-recurring nature for this purpose. 

(iii)	State Governments will advise their Universities to update / revise their curricula in technical education in consultation with AICTE, and also to switch over to semester and credit-based grading system.


(iv)	AICTE should be asked to review the norm fixed for polytechnics as also for
	introduction of new subjects so as to encourage private public participation. 

(v)	AICTE should also be asked to develop suitable mechanisms for meaningful and effective consultations with the State Governments. 

(vi)	The issue of having a minimum percentage of marks in the qualifying examination for admissions in the technical institutions was raised by the State Governments. It was noted that the AICTE Regulations in this regard do not specify such a percentage and all those who pass the qualifying examination are eligible to appear
in the., entrance examinations conducted for admissions to various technical
institutions. It was agreed that the AICTE would examine the feasibility of
imposing a condition of obtaining a minimum percentage of marks in the
qualifying examinations for entry in the technical institutions.


7	Vocational Education


Creation/upgradation of facilities which have a bearing on skills/training across all sectors of education need to be vigorously pursued. This would include, inter-alia, participation of trade / industry / employer organizations in curriculum design and certification.  A national framework of qualification for skills training would be devised.


8.    Distance Education


(i)	States agreed to liberally make available premises and other infrastructure of their institutions to serve as study-centres/regional centres of IGNOU, and to permit teachers in their institutions of higher learning to take up the assigned work in IGNOU Study Centres subject to the condition that their normal work should not be affected and IGNOU compensates the institutions at least to the extent of variable costs, besides giving some remuneration for the extra work done by the teachers.


(ii)	States noted the need to quickly enter into tripartite MoU with ISRO and MHRD for proper utilization of EDUSAT.  All States which are yet to execute these MoUs, agreed to do so in the next two months. States which have already executed the MeUs agreed to intensify efforts for providing infrastructural and administrative facilities for installation and maintenance of Satellite Interactive Terminals (SITs) and Receive Only Terminals (ROTs).


(iii)	States welcomed the establishment of the `SAKSHAT` Education Portal by the Ministry,. and agreed to host it on State-level servers as well, and also to develop
and post on this portal, learning modules in their respective languages, and suited
to the State-specific context.


9.	Minority Education


The Scheme of introduction of modern subjects in Madrassa institutions, being a demand-driven scheme, needs to be widely publicized with the active involvement of the State Governments. Special campaign would be launched by the MHRD, in association with State Governments, for willing Madrassa institutions to volunteer for assistance under the Scheme. The National Institute of Open Schooling 0`,lies) would launch a programme of linking with willing Madrassa Institutions for imparting education in modem subjects and vocational education through open and distance lecturing mode, leading to appropriate certification of eligible and successful Madrassa students by the NIOS.

title: LINKING OF EDUCATIONAL AND RESEARCH INSTITUTIONS IN THE COUNTRY .
question_text:
(a) whether there is any proposal under consideration of Government to link all educational and research institutions of higher learning in the country through a high speed Broadband Network to optimize the potential of institutions engaged in generation and dissemination of knowledge;

(b) whether his Ministry and National Knowledge Commission (NKC) have recommended linking of all educational and research institutions, Central universities, IITs and IIMs through a Broadband Network free of cost; and

(c) if so, how much this plan is likely to cost Government and expenditure required to be made in the Eleventh Five Year Plan?
answer_text:
MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SMT. D. PURANDESWARI)

(a) to (c): The University Grants Commission (UGC) has already provided connectivity to as many as 149 Universities, through the Information and Library Network Centre(INFLIBNET), an autonomous Inter University Centre of the UGC. Universities/educational institutions selected under the UGC’s INFONET Project have been provided High Speed Network Connectivity with 90% infrastructure cost and 100% recurring cost, out of grants provided to the Commission. During the XI th Five Year Plan, an amount of Rs. 192.64 crores will be required by the INFLIBNET to meet the expenditure for covering additional universities and colleges. Similarly, the Indian National Digital Library in Engineering Sciences and Technology (INDEST) Consortium provides networked access to electronic resources to 38 Centrally funded institutions including the IITs.

title: DIGITIZATION OF STUDY MATERIAL .
question_text:
(a) whether Government propose to digitize the study material for higher education;

(b) if so, the details thereof;

(c) what would be the financial implications for this scheme;

(d) which would be the nodal agencies to select the relevant material for digitization; and

(e) whether the scheme would also include the periodicals, as it may involve recurrent cost? A N S W E R
answer_text:
MINISTER OF HUMAN RESOURCE DEVELOPMENT

(SHRI ARJUN SINGH)

(a) to (e): A statement is laid on the Table of the House.

STATEMENT REFERRED TO IN REPLY TO PARTS (a) TO (e) OF RAJYA SABHA STARRED QUESTION NO.380 FOR 10.9.2007 ASKED BY SHRI KALRAJ MISHRA AND SHRI SANTOSH BAGRODIA REGARDING DIGITIZATION OF STUDY MATERIAL
